content:
5 min read
Just now

--

It’s not a Core Web Vital, it has no green badge to chase, and it’s silently dragging down the one metric Google does score you on.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Here’s a number most frontend developers never look at: Time to First Byte. It’s not in the Core Web Vitals club, so it gets ignored. But here’s the uncomfortable truth — every Core Web Vital starts after TTFB ends. If your server takes 1.8 seconds to send the first byte, your LCP can’t possibly be good no matter how perfectly you optimize images. TTFB is the floor under everything else, and almost nobody tunes it.
Let me change that.
What TTFB is, precisely
TTFB measures the time from when the browser starts requesting a page to when it receives the very first byte of the response. Not the whole document — just byte one. It’s the gap between “I asked” and “the server started answering.”
Google’s rough guidance: aim for TTFB under 800ms at the 75th percentile, and ideally well below that. It’s classified as a diagnostic Web Vital — a number you use to explain why a Core metric (especially LCP) is bad, rather than one you’re graded on directly.
TTFB is not one thing — it’s a chain
The mistake people make is treating TTFB as “server speed.” It’s actually a sequence of steps, and a slow TTFB could be any link in the chain. Knowing the breakdown tells you where to look:

• Redirects — each 301/302 is a full extra round trip before the real request even starts.

• DNS lookup — resolving your domain to an IP.

• Connection + TLS handshake — opening the socket and negotiating HTTPS.

• Request travel time — the request crossing the network to your origin.

• Server processing — the backend actually building the response (DB queries, rendering, API calls).

• Response travel time — the first byte coming back to the browser.
You can see this exact breakdown for any request in DevTools → Network → click a request → Timing tab. The “Waiting for server response” (TTFB) row is highlighted, with the connection and “Initial connection” phases above it. Read that before you assume it’s your backend — half the time it’s a redirect or distance, not slow code.
Or grab it programmatically:
new PerformanceObserver((list) => {
const nav = list.getEntries()[0];
console.log('TTFB:', Math.round(nav.responseStart), 'ms');
console.log(' redirect:', Math.round(nav.redirectEnd - nav.redirectStart), 'ms');
console.log(' dns:', Math.round(nav.domainLookupEnd - nav.domainLookupStart), 'ms');
console.log(' connect:', Math.round(nav.connectEnd - nav.connectStart), 'ms');
}).observe({ type: 'navigation', buffered: true });How TTFB poisons LCP
This is the connection that makes TTFB worth your time. Remember that LCP is the sum of four phases, and the very first one is TTFB. If LCP is “render the hero by 2.5s,” and TTFB alone eats 1.8s, you have 700ms left for the browser to discover, download, and paint a hero image — which is nearly impossible on mobile.
So when someone’s LCP is stuck and they’ve already optimized their images, the first question Frontend Master always asks is: “What’s your TTFB?” More often than not, the document is arriving late and there’s nothing the frontend can do to compensate. (If you’re chasing an LCP problem, read the LCP deep dive alongside this — the two are joined at the hip.)
The fixes, by where the time is going
1. Kill redirects
Each redirect is a wasted round trip. Common silent offenders:

• http:// → https:// then non-www → www — that's two redirects on every first visit.

• Old marketing URLs that bounce through 2–3 hops.
Fix: link directly to the final canonical URL. Configure HSTS so browsers go straight to HTTPS. Collapse redirect chains to at most one hop.
2. Put a CDN in front
If your server is in Virginia and your user is in Mumbai, every byte crosses an ocean. Steps 4 and 6 (request/response travel) are dominated by physical distance.
Fix: a CDN serves cached responses from a node near the user. For static and cacheable pages this can take TTFB from 800ms to under 100ms, because the request never reaches your origin at all.
3. Cache the response
The slowest possible TTFB is one where the server rebuilds the entire page from scratch — database queries, template rendering, third-party API calls — on every single request.
Fixes:

• Full-page / edge caching for content that doesn’t change per user.

• CDN caching with sensible Cache-Control headers so repeat and nearby visits skip the origin.
Cache-Control: public, max-age=300, s-maxage=86400, stale-while-revalidate=600stale-while-revalidate is the underrated one — it serves a slightly stale cached response instantly (great TTFB) while refreshing in the background, so users almost never wait on a cache miss.
4. Speed up the server itself (step 5)
When processing time is genuinely the bottleneck:

• Add indexes to slow queries; eliminate N+1 query patterns.

• Cache expensive computations and API calls.

• For SSR frameworks, stream the response so the first byte goes out before the whole page is rendered — streaming literally improves TTFB by definition.

• Move work off the critical path: don’t block the response on analytics or non-essential calls.
5. Modern transport
Use HTTP/2 or HTTP/3 and keep TLS efficient (session resumption, OCSP stapling). These shave milliseconds off the connection phase that add up across the chain.
Why “nobody optimizes” it — and why that’s an edge
TTFB sits in the gap between frontend and backend. Frontend devs assume it’s the backend’s job; backend devs assume the page is fine because their API is fast. So it falls through the cracks. That’s exactly why fixing it is high-leverage: it’s often the single biggest, least-contested win available, and it lifts LCP for free.
The takeaway
TTFB is the quiet tax on every page load. It won’t show up as a failing Core Web Vital with a red badge, but it caps how good your LCP can ever be. Check the Network Timing tab, find which link in the chain is slow — redirect, distance, or processing — and fix that link. CDN and caching solve most cases in an afternoon.
For how TTFB feeds the bigger picture, the Core Web Vitals beginner intro maps out where this diagnostic metric sits relative to LCP, INP, and CLS.
Watch the full walkthrough
